items.html: Discuss what sickle cell disease is and the 2 hallmark components
Identify opportunities for partnership with PCPs in the monitoring and dose escalation for Hydroxyurea

data.html:
How Do I Diagnose SCD?

Which of the following is a common symptom of sickle cell disease?

Fatigue

How is Sickle Cell Disease diagnosed?

Through a blood test, called a hemoglobin electrophoresis.

HbF concentrations start to decrease after birth, around 4-6 months of age and this is when infants are more likely to start showing signs of VOE, since their HbS is now on the rise.

For infants, VOE presents itself as swollen hands and feet. This is called dactylitis.

During childhood and early adolescence, there are common delays in physical growth and sexual development.

Patients with SCD tend to have relatively low body fat and as such they are usually thin.
Patients on chronic transfusions are most likely receiving iron chelation therapy as well.

slides.title: The Essential Role of the PCP in Hydroxyurea (HU) Management

data.html:
Hydroxyurea has been used in patients with SCD for many decades and has helped to reduce pain crisis, hospitalizations, and emergency department visits, instead of just treating the pain.

HU is a once a day medication that is fairly inexpensive, easy to take, extensively studied, and readily available in most developed countries.

It is an anti neoplastic agent that can cause myelosuppression and as such requires close monitoring of lab values.

Approved in 1998 by FDA for use in SCD in adults. Has not been approved by Health Canada.

HU inhibits ribonucleotide reductase, a DNA synthesis enzyme. This causes haematopoiesis to be interrupted and leads to stress erythropoiesis in bone marrow.

The bone marrow starts producing the earlier progenitor erythrocyte cells that still contain HbF.

HbF does not adhere to other Hb molecules, which interrupts polymerization, effectively reducing HbS.

Target HbF: >20%

Reaching Maximum Tolerated Dose

Primary care providers are in the position to help initiate, monitor, and titrate their patient's HU using the tables below and involving haematology for consultation.

Side Effects • gastrointestinal problems • headache • drowsiness • skin and nail changes • Rare occasions - reports of hallucinations and seizures • Long-term use of hydroxyurea (> 3 years) may induce leg ulcers

Mortality Rates HU reduces mortality rates: • Adults - 40% reduced mortality • Children – 15-year survival rate is 99.4%, which is 4% higher than children not taking HU – 95.4%)

Cancer Risk • The literature supports this as the biggest fear of PCPs around the use of HU • Fear their patient will develop cancer • 27% of pediatric providers • 40% of adult providers • Despite the substantial data and clinical experience to date that contradicts those concerns

Pregnancy • Women should discontinue HU, once they are confirmed to be pregnant • Men can continue to take HU while trying to conceive with partner

data.heading: With an understanding of hydroxyurea, PCPs could assist by:

items.html: help with monitoring tolerance and dose
Sickle Cell Disease is a complex disease that requires the collaboration of multiple disciplines to optimize care outcomes and experiences.
The two hallmark components of SCD are hemolysis and vaso-occlusion
PCPs are integral to the early identification of complications
Monitoring bloodwork and adjusting doses of hydroxyurea, by the PCP, will give patients the best chance at being successful in maintaining their regimen.
Biodigital Human: Explore the Body in 3D. Retrieved from: https://human.biodigital.com/widgets/novartis-scd/
Hehre, R. 2020. Managing patients with sickle cell disease in primary care Robert Journal of the American Academy of PAs
Moawad, H. 2023. Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia. Retrieved from: https://www.verywellhealth.com/sickle-cell-anemia-7507053
Onimoe, G., & Rotz, S. 2020. Sickle cell disease: A primary care update. Cleveland Clinic Journal of Medicine, 87(1)
Rees, D., Williams, T., & Gladwin, M. 2010. Sickle Cell Disease. Seminar 376 (9757), 2018-2031.
Lai, R. 2024. The Role of the Primary Care Physician in SCD. Rare disease advisor.
